description: Standard: feature article on gig economy

input.article_text:
The app told Marcus Reed he would earn $28 for the delivery. By the time he reached the restaurant, the order had been cancelled and reassigned. He received $3.50 for the trip — gas money, roughly, if he drove a car that got better mileage than his.

Gig workers in the city have no guaranteed minimum wage, no benefits, and no recourse when an algorithm changes the terms of their labor mid-shift. What they have is flexibility — a word that appears in every platform's marketing material and that every worker interviewed for this story used with varying degrees of irony.

'Flexibility means I get to choose which 14-hour day I work,' said Priya Sharma, who has driven for three different platforms simultaneously since 2023. 'That is not the same as freedom.'

A 2025 study from the Labor Policy Institute found that the median gig worker in the city earns $11.40 per hour after expenses — below the federal minimum wage and roughly half the city's living wage threshold.

The platforms dispute these figures, arguing that the study does not account for the value of schedule autonomy.
The council voted 5-2 to approve the new zoning ordinance. 'This is a win for responsible development,' said Council President Angela Torres.

description: European: article on EU digital identity

input.article_text:
By 2027, every EU citizen will have access to a European Digital Identity wallet — a government-issued app that stores ID documents, driving licences, university diplomas, and medical prescriptions. The ambition is seamless: cross a border, rent a car, open a bank account, all with a tap.

'We are building the infrastructure of trust for the digital age,' said Thierry Breton, when he was still Commissioner for Internal Market. What he did not say, and what privacy advocates have been warning about since the regulation was first proposed, is that the wallet creates a single point of surveillance.

'If the government issues the credential and the government verifies the credential, then the government sees every transaction,' said digital rights lawyer Estelle Massé. 'That is not trust. That is tracking.'

The regulation passed with broad support. The concerns, as is often the case in Brussels, were noted and overridden.
